coredata.dc:description: Malachite green (MG), a synthetic dye widely used in industrial applications and aquaculture, poses significant health hazards due to its genotoxic and carcinogenic properties. Addressing these risks requires innovative methods for the efficient MG removal and sensitive detection. In this study, we developed a novel β-cyclodextrin/carbon dots-loaded cellulose nanofibrils/MIL-100(Fe) hydrogel (βCCM) for enhanced adsorption and fluorescence detection of MG. The βCCM demonstrated an exceptional maximum adsorption capacity of 1789 mg/g. Furthermore, βCCM exhibited bright orange fluorescence, which was significantly quenched upon exposure to MG through inner filter effect and static quenching. The fluorescence detection platform exhibited high sensitivity, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.09 μg/L and excellent linearity. Its practical applicability was validated through successful MG removal and detection in real water samples, achieving high recoveries and reproducibility. Mechanistic investigations revealed that the enhanced adsorption performance resulted from the incorporation of MIL-100(Fe) and the formation of a three-dimensional (3D) porous structure, which also stabilized the fluorescent signal and amplified detection sensitivity. This study highlights the effectiveness of βCCM for both MG adsorption and detection, presenting a novel strategy for designing advanced β-cyclodextrin-based hydrogels tailored for water treatment.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent β-cyclodextrin-based hydrogel for enhanced adsorption and fluorescence detection of malachite green

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Malachite green (MG), a synthetic dye widely used in industrial applications and aquaculture, poses significant health hazards due to its genotoxic and carcinogenic properties. Addressing these risks requires innovative methods for the efficient MG removal and sensitive detection. In this study, we developed a novel β-cyclodextrin/carbon dots-loaded cellulose nanofibrils/MIL-100(Fe) hydrogel (βCCM) for enhanced adsorption and fluorescence detection of MG. The βCCM demonstrated an exceptional maximum adsorption capacity of 1789 mg/g. Furthermore, βCCM exhibited bright orange fluorescence, which was significantly quenched upon exposure to MG through inner filter effect and static quenching. The fluorescence detection platform exhibited high sensitivity, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.09 μg/L and excellent linearity. Its practical applicability was validated through successful MG removal and detection in real water samples, achieving high recoveries and reproducibility. Mechanistic investigations revealed that the enhanced adsorption performance resulted from the incorporation of MIL-100(Fe) and the formation of a three-dimensional (3D) porous structure, which also stabilized the fluorescent signal and amplified detection sensitivity. This study highlights the effectiveness of βCCM for both MG adsorption and detection, presenting a novel strategy for designing advanced β-cyclodextrin-based hydrogels tailored for water treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Malachite green (MG), a synthetic dye belonging to the triphenylmethane family, is widely used in various industries, including textiles, paper, and leather, as well as in aquaculture [1]. However, its widespread application raises significant environmental and public health concerns. MG is known to have teratogenic, carcinogenic, and mutagenic effects, posing serious risks to human health and ecosystems [2]. Therefore, the removal of MG from contaminated wastewater is critical to mitigate its adverse impacts on the environment and human health.

Numerous methods have been explored for MG removal from polluted water, including adsorption, membrane filtration, and biological treatment [3]. Among these, adsorption has garnered significant attention due to its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and environmental compatibility. The efficiency of adsorption largely depends on the properties of the adsorbents. Traditional adsorbents, such as activated carbon and natural zeolite, have been extensively studied; however, their practical application is often hindered by limitations, including high costs, low adsorption capacities, and difficulties in separation and regeneration [4]. As sustainable development goals emphasize eco-friendly and efficient solutions, the development of advanced adsorbents for MG removal remains a critical challenge.

β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), a cyclic oligosaccharide derived from starch, has emerged as a promising candidate for wastewater treatment due to its biodegradability, renewability, and ability to encapsulate hydrophobic molecules within its cavity [5]. β-CD-based hydrogels, in particular, have shown great potential as efficient adsorbents for MG. For example, Ren et al. developed a biodegradable β-CD/gelatin/chitosan hydrogel capable of removing MG from aqueous solutions [6], while Li et al. fabricated a β-CD/magnetic graphene oxide-functionalized polyacrylic acid hydrogel that achieved a 95 % removal efficiency for 50 mg/L of MG [7]. Despite these advances, the relatively low adsorption capacities of β-CD-based hydrogels remain a challenge. Incorporating nanomaterials with superior adsorption properties offers a promising strategy to address this limitation. Among nanomaterials, cellulose nanofibrils (CN) and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted considerable attention due to their remarkable physicochemical properties. CN have a high aspect ratio, large surface area, and abundant functional groups, enabling the formation of 3D porous structures and providing numerous binding sites for adsorption [8]. MOFs, particularly MIL-100(Fe), exhibit exceptional adsorption properties due to their unique porous architecture, high surface area, and excellent chemical stability. MIL-100(Fe) is also distinguished by its low cost and non-toxic nature, making it an ideal component for environmentally friendly adsorbents [9]. The synergistic integration of CN and MIL-100(Fe) into β-CD-based hydrogels holds substantial promise for enhancing MG adsorption performance.

In addition to removal, the detection of MG in contaminated water is essential for effective monitoring and risk assessment. Conventional detection methods, such as liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry, and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays, offer high accuracy but are often limited by their complexity, time-consuming protocols, and high operational costs [10]. Fluorescent probes have emerged as a powerful alternative, providing rapid, cost-effective, and sensitive detection [11]. Among these, carbon dots (CD) have garnered increasing interest due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and ease of synthesis [12]. For instance, Du et al. reported the synthesis of CD via a one-step hydrothermal method for detecting MG [13]. However, CD are prone to agglomeration, which can result in aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching, compromising detection accuracy [14]. Embedding CD into a robust carrier material, such as β-CD-based hydrogels, can mitigate this issue by stabilizing their fluorescence properties and preventing aggregation.

Given the unique properties of β-CD, CN, MIL-100(Fe), and CD, their combination offers a promising approach for developing multifunctional materials capable of simultaneous MG adsorption and detection. However, the synergistic effects of these components on adsorption and detection performance remain unexplored. In this study, we hypothesized that integrating these components into a single material would enable the development of a novel multifunctional hydrogel, termed βCCM. To test this hypothesis, we synthesized βCCM and systematically characterized its structural, chemical, and optical properties using techniques such as scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The adsorption performance of βCCM was evaluated under various conditions, including pH, temperature (T), initial MG concentration (C0), and contact time (t), while its fluorescence-based detection capability was assessed in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, and practicality using real water samples. Finally, we elucidated the mechanisms underlying the adsorption and fluorescence detection of MG by βCCM, offering insights into its multifunctional capabilities.

Acrylic acid (AA, 99 %), potassium persulfate (PPS, ≥99 %), homophthalic acid (98 %), nickel chloride hexahydrate (≥98 %), copper chloride, and copper chloride dihydrate were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. N, N’-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 99.5 %) was sourced from Hefei BASF Biotechnology Co., Ltd. β-CD (98 %) and MG were purchased from Tianjin Hynes Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

CN were prepared following a previously reported 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy free radical (TEMPO)-mediated oxidation protocol with slight modifications [15]. Bleached wood pulp (BWP, 10.0 g) was suspended in 300 mL of deionized water, and a separate solution (Solution A) was prepared by dissolving 0.16 g of TEMPO and 1.03 g of NaBr in 300 mL of water. Solution A was gradually added to the BWP suspension, followed by the addition of 60 mmol of NaClO. The pH of the mixture was maintained at 10 to facilitate the oxidation reaction, which proceeded for 6 h under stirring. Afterward, the reaction mixture was neutralized by washing with deionized water and subjected to high-pressure homogenization for five cycles. The resulting CN were dialyzed against deionized water for 4 days.

CD were synthesized via a hydrothermal method as described in previous studies [16]. Briefly, 0.50 g of rhodamine B was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water within a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined reactor. The mixture was heated at 160 °C for 2 h and subsequently allowed to cool to room temperature. The resulting dark brown solution was filtered and dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 48 h, yielding purified CD.

Carbon dots-loaded cellulose nanofibrils (CD@CN) were prepared using a modified procedure from the literature [17]. A suspension of CN (10 g, 0.1 wt%) was mixed with varying volumes of CD solution (25, 50, 75, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, and 1600 μL). The mixture was stirred at 25 °C in the dark for 24 h to ensure uniform loading of CD onto the CN. The product was dialyzed for 48 h and designated as CD@CN-x, where x represents the volume of CD added, corresponding to values from 1 to 9.

MIL-100(Fe) was synthesized using a solvothermal method [18]. Initially, 9.6544 g of FeCl3·6H2O was dissolved in 36 mL of deionized water and stirred mechanically at 25 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, 5.04 g of trimellitic acid was added to the solution, and the mixture was stirred for an additional hour. The resulting pink-orange solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was washed thoroughly. The purified MIL-100(Fe) was dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C.

To optimize the hydrogel composition for maximum MG adsorption capacity (Qe), an L9(34) orthogonal experimental design [19] was employed to evaluate key variables: MIL-100(Fe) (A), AA (B), NaOH (C), and MBA (D). The parameter levels and statistical analysis results are presented in Tables S1 and S2.

The optimized procedure for βCCM-x synthesis involved dissolving 0.1 g of β-CD in deionized water at 75 °C, followed by the addition of 5 mg of MIL-100(Fe) with ultrasound-assisted mixing. Subsequently, 1.8 g of CD@CN-6, 2.0 g of AA, 1.6 mL of 5 mol/L NaOH solution, 0.02 g of MBA, and 0.01 g of PPS were sequentially added to the solution. The mixture was sonicated to achieve homogeneity and then transferred to a glass test tube for polymerization at 60 °C in a water bath for 1 h. The resultant hydrogel was designated as βCCM-6. Control hydrogels (βCCM-x) were prepared following the same procedure, with CD@CN-x (x = 1 to 9) incorporated as specified.

Adsorption experiments were conducted to evaluate the performance of the prepared hydrogels in removing MG. Wet hydrogel samples (20 mg) were added to 100 mL of MG solution and stirred constantly at 150 rpm. Unless otherwise specified, the C0 was 50 mg/L, pH was set to 6, the t was 240 min, and the T was maintained at 25 °C. Adsorption isotherms were determined by varying MG concentration (50–1000 mg/L), and adsorption kinetics were evaluated by monitoring contact time (30–240 min). The effect of pH on adsorption was examined within the range of 4–8, while temperature-dependent adsorption studies were conducted at 15–55 °C. After adsorption, the hydrogels were separated from the solution, and residual MG concentrations were quantified using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hanon Instruments Co., Model I3, China) at the maximum absorption wavelength of 617 nm.

The fluorescence sensing capabilities of the prepared samples for MG were evaluated by immersing the samples in solutions with varying MG concentrations. After a predetermined detection time, the samples were carefully removed, and any residual liquid on their surfaces was gently wiped off. Subsequently, the absolute quantum yield and fluorescence spectra of the samples were measured.

To assess selectivity, thirteen dyes—Methylene Blue (M B), Rhodamine B (RHB), Crystal Violet (CV), Leuco-Crystal Violet (LCV), Brilliant Green (BG), Leuco-Malachite Green (LMG), Methyl Orange (MO), Sunset Yellow (SY), Tartrazine (TZ), Orange G (OG), Amaranth (AM), Eosin Y (EY), Safranine O (SO)—four amino acids—arginine (ARG), citrulline (CIT), histidine (HIS), and cysteine (CYS)—and four metal ions—Cu2+, Na+, Ni2+, and K+—were used as interfering substances for MG in fluorescence detection experiments.

Water samples were collected from three reservoirs in Changsha City, Hunan Province, China: Datang Reservoir, Shuntang Reservoir, and Dongfeng Reservoir. Sampling locations were strategically selected to include the four corners and a diagonal cross-section of each reservoir. Samples were collected at a depth of 50 cm from the water surface and 1 m from the edge. Five samples from each reservoir were pooled, stored in clean polyethylene bottles, and promptly transported to the laboratory for analysis.

Sample Pretreatment: The collected water samples were pretreated by filtration through double-layered gauze to remove large impurities, followed by filtration through a 0.22 µm membrane. The filtered water was then immediately used to prepare MG solutions at concentrations ranging from 20 to 100 mg/L for subsequent adsorption and detection experiments.

The morphological features of the samples were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Tescan Mira3, Czech Republic). Chemical structures were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Model 100, PerkinElmer, USA). Crystalline structures were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a JEOL D8 diffractometer (Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was conducted using a luminescence spectrometer (F97-Pro, Shanghai Prismatic Technology Co., Ltd., China).

Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation process of βCCM, which integrates β-CD, CD, CN, and MIL-100(Fe) as key constituents. Initially, CD were synthesized via a hydrothermal method [16], while CN were prepared through a combination of TEMPO-mediated oxidation and high-pressure homogenization [15]. Subsequently, CD were anchored onto CN through hydrogen bonding interactions, forming CD@CN [20]. In parallel, MIL-100(Fe) was synthesized by hydrothermally reacting metal ions with organic ligands in an aqueous medium [18]. To construct βCCM, β-CD, CD@CN, the monomer AA, MIL-100(Fe), and the crosslinker MBA were homogeneously mixed. Polymerization was initiated by adding PPS, leading to the formation of a 3D porous structure [21]. The incorporation of CN promoted the construction of the 3D porous structure [8], while MIL-100(Fe) introduced abundant adsorption sites [18]. CD endowed the material with rapid and selective fluorescence response capabilities [12]. These synergistic structural features collectively enhanced the performance of βCCM for MG adsorption and detection [22].

The microstructures of CN, CD@CN, MIL-100(Fe), and βCCM-6 were thoroughly characterized. SEM analysis of CN revealed their smooth surface morphology (Fig. 2 a) [23]. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CD showed uniformly dispersed nanoparticles (Fig. 2b) [16], while high-resolution TEM (Fig. S1) confirmed a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphene [24]. The surface of CD@CN appeared rough and fractured, indicating successful CD loading onto CN (Fig. 2c) [25]. Additionally, the SEM image of MIL-100(Fe) (Fig. S2) revealed its octahedral crystalline structure with an average diameter of ∼ 0.25 μm [26]. In contrast, βCCM-6 exhibited a 3D porous structure (Fig. 2d), providing numerous active sites for adsorption. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping confirmed the uniform distribution of C, N, O, and Fe within βCCM-6, verifying the homogeneous incorporation of CD and MIL-100(Fe) (Fig. S3) [27].

FTIR spectroscopy was employed to identify the functional groups present in β-CD, CN, CD@CN, MIL-100(Fe), and βCCM-6 (Fig. 2e). Characteristic peaks of β-CD included OH stretching (3308 cm−1), CH stretching (2921 cm−1), and COC stretching (1017 cm−1) [28]. CN and CD@CN exhibited peaks at 3308 cm−1 and 2927 cm−1, corresponding to H-bond and CH stretching, respectively, along with bands at 1605, 1418, 1345, and 1025 cm−1 associated with symmetric and asymmetric stretching of carboxyl groups and CO stretching [29]. The presence of a CN peak at 1087 cm−1 in CD@CN confirmed hydrogen bonding between CD and CN [20]. MIL-100(Fe) displayed distinct bands for the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of COOH groups at 1637 cm−1 and 1534 cm−1, along with characteristic FeO tensile vibrations at 466 cm−1 [30]. For βCCM-6, the characteristic bands of β-CD and CD@CN were notably reduced, suggesting their involvement in the free radical polymerization reaction. Meanwhile, the intensities of the MIL-100(Fe) peaks decreased, likely due to the incorporation of MIL-100(Fe) into βCCM-6 [22].

Fig. 2f presents the XRD patterns of β-CD, CN, CD@CN, MIL-100(Fe), and βCCM-6. β-CD exhibited distinct diffraction peaks at 9.2°, 10.8°, 12.7°, 15.6°, 17.3°, 19.1°, 19.8°, 20.9°, 23.0°, 27.4°, 31.3°, 32.1°, and 35.0°, indicating a predominantly cage-like aggregation [31]. MIL-100(Fe) displayed seven distinct peaks at 2θ = 6.8°, 10.8°, 14.8°, 20.1°, 24.8°, and 28.2°. CN and CD@CN showed characteristic cellulose peaks at 2θ = 16.4° and 22.4°. CD@CN did not exhibit any new peaks compared to CN, suggesting that CD loading via hydrogen bonding did not alter the crystal structure [32]. However, these peaks from β-CD and CD@CN in βCCM-6 were diminished, indicating their involvement in free radical polymerization. The peaks of MIL-100(Fe) were also almost absent, likely due to its incorporation into βCCM-6 [33].

Fig. 2 g illustrates the UV–vis and fluorescence properties of βCCM-6. An absorbance peak at 260 nm was attributed to the π–π* transition of the CC sp2 domains in the carbon core [34]. The optimal excitation wavelength for βCCM-6 was 498 nm, corresponding to an emission wavelength of 597 nm. PL spectra of βCCM-6 at different excitation wavelengths were also analyzed (Fig. 2 h). The PL emission peaks of βCCM-6 remained consistent across excitation wavelengths, demonstrating excitation-independent fluorescence behavior [35].

Photographs of βCCM-x under UV light (365 nm) vividly illustrated fluorescence variations with varying CD content (Fig. 2i). Fluorescence intensity initially increased with higher CD loading before declining, with βCCM-6 exhibiting the strongest fluorescence and an absolute quantum yield (A0) of 8.45 %. Furthermore, βCCM-6 demonstrated excellent stability, retaining its fluorescence intensity and A0 after one month of storage at 4 °C (Fig. S4), attributed to the protective effect of its 3D porous structure.

The response surface methodology (RSM) combined with the Box–Behnken design was employed to evaluate the effects of independent variables on the Qe of βCCM-6 [36]. A total of 17 experimental trials were conducted under specified conditions, with the results summarized in Table S3. Analysis of variance (ANOVA; Table S4) revealed that T, pH, and C0 significantly affected Qe, as indicated by low p-values and high F-values [37]. These variables were further visualized through 3D response surface plots (Fig. 3 a–c), which highlighted their influence. Notably, the impact of the variables followed the order: C0 > T > pH. Within the design range, Qe increased with rising T and C0, while an initial increase in pH led to a peak in Qe, followed by a decline. The optimal conditions for achieving Qe > 100 mg/g were determined as 5 < pH < 7, T > 25 °C, and C0 > 50 mg/L, emphasizing the sensitivity of βCCM-6 to environmental variations [38].

The adsorption behavior of βCCM-6 varied significantly with pH (Fig. 3d). Qe increased steadily as the pH rose from 4 to 6, reaching its maximum at pH 6. However, a subsequent decline in Qe was observed as the pH was further increased to 8. This behavior was explained by the isoelectric point (pHzpc) of βCCM-6, experimentally determined to be 2.4 (Fig. S5) [39]. As the pH increased from 4 to 6, the surface of βCCM-6 became progressively more negatively charged, enhancing electrostatic attraction with the cationic MG molecules and leading to a corresponding increase in Qe [40]. At pH values above 6, two factors contributed to the decline in Qe. First, the concentration of hydroxyl ions in solution increased, resulting in competitive interactions at the active adsorption sites of βCCM-6. Second, the net negative charge of βCCM-6 was partially mitigated at higher pH levels, reducing the electrostatic driving force for MG adsorption. These results indicated that the optimal pH for adsorption was 6.

As shown in Fig. 3e, Qe increased with temperature, ranging from 15 °C to 55 °C, indicating the endothermic nature of the process [41]. Thermodynamic parameters calculated using standard equations (Fig. S6) supported this observation. The positive enthalpy change (ΔH = 38.48 kJ/mol) confirmed the endothermic nature, while the positive entropy change (ΔS = 11.96 J/mol·K) suggested increased system disorder during adsorption [42]. Additionally, the negative Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) across the studied temperature range indicated a spontaneous adsorption process [43].

The effect of C0 on adsorption behavior was investigated (Fig. 3f). As C0 increased, Qe of βCCM-6 rose rapidly before reaching a plateau, indicating saturation. Notably, βCCM-6 exhibited higher Qe values compared to βCC and βC under identical conditions, underscoring the synergistic enhancement conferred by CD and MIL-100(Fe) [44]. To further elucidate the adsorption process, both Langmuir and Freundlich models were applied to the data. Fig. 3 h and 3i showed that the Langmuir model had a higher correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9118) compared to the Freundlich model (R2 = 0.8744). These results indicated that the adsorption of MG by βCCM-6 followed the Langmuir model, suggesting monolayer chemisorption [45]. Based on the Langmuir model, the maximum adsorption capacity (Qm) was calculated to be 1789.4 mg/g, closely aligning with the experimentally determined saturation capacity of 1626.7 mg/g. Comparative analysis highlighted βCCM-6 as one of the most efficient adsorbents for MG, as summarized in Table S5.

The adsorption process was time-dependent, with the adsorption capacity over time (Qt) rapidly increasing before reaching equilibrium (Fig. 3 g). Notably, βCCM-6 consistently exhibited superior kinetic performance compared to βCC and βC, which can be attributed to the enhanced active sites provided by CD and MIL-100(Fe) [46]. Kinetic analysis using both pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models indicated that the latter provided a better fit, as evidenced by higher R2 values (Fig. 3 h–i). This suggested that chemisorption predominantly governed the adsorption process of βCCM-6.

To verify the reliability of βCCM-6, adsorption experiments were conducted using three actual water samples. As shown in Table S6, the Qe values for βCCM-6 in water samples from Datang Reservoir, Shuntang Reservoir, and Dongfeng Reservoir decreased by 21.1 %, 10.0 %, and 6.7 %, respectively, compared to the initial Qe in deionized water. Despite the reduction in adsorption capacity in real water samples, βCCM-6 still demonstrated significant adsorption capacity for MG, confirming its feasibility for practical applications [47].

Water contaminated with MG often contains various additional pollutants, which may interfere with the adsorption performance of βCCM-6 due to competitive interactions [48]. To assess the separation efficiency of βCCM-6 for selectively adsorbing MG, we performed experiments in a single-component solution at pH 6. Following previous studies [48], eight contaminants—MO, SY, TZ, OG, AM, EY, CV, and SO—were selected, each at an initial concentration of 50 mg/L, with a contact time of 240 min. As shown in Fig. S7, βCCM-6 exhibited minimal adsorption of most contaminants. In contrast, it demonstrated significantly higher adsorption efficiency for MG. The selective adsorption capability of βCCM-6, emphasizing its separation efficiency, was further corroborated by the notably high Kd value for MG (Fig. S8). These results highlighted the exceptional separation property of βCCM-6 for the selective adsorption of MG.

The adsorption mechanism of βCCM-6 was elucidated through FTIR and XPS analyses, revealing key interactions between βCCM-6 and MG. The FTIR spectra of βCCM-6 before and after adsorption exhibited significant changes (Fig. S9a). After adsorption, two new characteristic bands appeared at 1349 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1, corresponding to the CN groups of MG and the CC bonds in its sp2 carbon framework, respectively, confirming successful adsorption [49]. Additionally, the O–H stretching vibration band at 3308 cm−1 diminished, suggesting the formation of hydrogen bonds between βCCM-6 and MG [50]. The reduction in the intensity of the CO band at 1637 cm−1 further implied electrostatic interactions between the carboxyl groups of βCCM-6 and the amino groups of MG [51].

XPS analysis provided additional insights into the adsorption mechanism (Fig. S9b). After MG adsorption, the N1s spectrum showed an enhanced nitrogen peak, confirming the successful adsorption of nitrogen-rich MG onto βCCM-6 [52]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S9c) revealed shifts and intensity changes in the peaks at 284.55 eV (CC), 286.47 eV (CO), and 288.57 eV (CO). Notably, the increased intensity of the CC peak highlighted π–π interactions between the benzene rings of MG and the conjugated structure of βCCM-6 [52]. In the O1s spectrum (Fig. S9d), peaks at 531.76 eV (COH) and 533.04 eV (CO) shifted after adsorption, accompanied by an increase in the COH peak intensity, further indicating the role of hydrogen bonding in MG adsorption [53]. Moreover, the N1s spectrum (Fig. S9e) revealed a shift in the CN peak from 399.17 eV to 399.01 eV and the –NH3 + peak from 400.75 eV to 400.39 eV, corroborating electrostatic interactions between the protonated amino groups (–NH3 +) of MG and βCCM-6 [54]. Finally, the Fe2p spectrum (Fig. S9f) displayed peaks at 711.03 eV (Fe2p3/2) and 724.60 eV (Fe2p1/2) before adsorption, which shifted to lower binding energies post-adsorption, indicating the formation of metal- N bonds [54].
Collectively, these findings confirmed that MG adsorption onto βCCM-6 was governed by a combination of π–π interaction, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, and metal- N coordination.

The selectivity of βCCM-6 for MG was systematically evaluated using various potential interfering substances, including thirteen dyes (M B, RHB, CV, LCV, BG, LMG, MO, SY, TZ, OG, AM, EO, SO, as shown in Fig. 4 ), four amino acids (ARG, CIT, HIS, and CYS, as shown in Fig. S10), and four metal ions (Cu2+, Na+, Ni2+, and K+, as shown in Fig. S10) [55]. As illustrated in Fig. 4a and Fig. S10, only MG caused significant fluorescence quenching of βCCM-6, while all other substances had negligible effects. This pronounced fluorescence quenching was visually evident under UV light, where βCCM-6 treated with MG exhibited a marked darkening compared to other substances. The selectivity was quantified by the fluorescence change, expressed as the ratio of Ax to A0, where Ax represents the absolute quantum yield of βCCM-6 with the interfering substance (Fig. 4b) [56]. Among all tested substances, MG induced the largest decrease in the Ax/A0 ratio, highlighting the exceptional selectivity of βCCM-6 for MG.

To optimize the detection sensitivity of βCCM-6 for MG, three key parameters—temperature, detection time, and pH—were investigated [57]. As shown in Fig. S11, the fluorescence intensity of βCCM-6 remained stable across a temperature range of 15–55 °C. However, the fluorescence response to MG increased with temperature. For practical purposes, 25 °C was chosen for subsequent experiments. Detection time optimization studies (Fig. S12) revealed that fluorescence quenching occurred rapidly within 1 min of MG addition and stabilized after 2 min, establishing 2 min as the optimal detection time. Furthermore, pH optimization (Fig. S13) demonstrated that the fluorescence response was most pronounced at pH 6, suggesting it as the ideal pH for detection. Based on these results, the optimal detection conditions were determined to be 25 °C, 2 min, and pH 6.

Under the optimized conditions, βCCM-6 exhibited significant fluorescence quenching upon the addition of MG. As shown in Fig. 5 a, fluorescence images under UV excitation (365 nm) revealed a progressive quenching effect as the C0 increased. Quantitative analysis indicated that the absolute quantum yield (Ax) of βCCM-6 decreased by 99 % over an MG concentration range of 20–100 mg/L, with a strong linear correlation (Ax = − 0.0133 × C0 + 8.5049, R2 = 0.9914).

At lower MG concentrations (0.2–1.0 μg/L), a linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity change (I0 − I)/I and C0 was also observed (Fig. 5b). The fitted equation, (I0 − I)/I = 0.0768 × C0-0.0016 (R2 = 0.9905), enabled precise quantification of MG, with a LOD calculated to be 0.09 μg/L [58]. Here, I0 and I represented the fluorescence intensities of βCCM-6 before and after MG addition, respectively.

To assess the performance of βCCM-6, its MG detection capabilities, including linear range and LOD, were compared with those reported in previous studies (Table 1 ). The results revealed that βCCM-6 achieved an LOD comparable to or better than those of previously reported materials, while offering a broader detection range [17]. This combination of sensitivity and selectivity demonstrated the potential of βCCM-6 for practical MG detection applications [59].

To evaluate the practical applicability of βCCM-6, its detection performance in real water samples was assessed using the standard addition method [67]. The results are summarized in Table S7. For real water samples, βCCM-6 demonstrated excellent recovery rates, ranging from 91.54 % to 109.14 %, with standard deviations below 4.92 %. These findings confirmed the reliability and accuracy of βCCM-6 for MG quantification in complex real-world matrices, highlighting its potential for practical applications in environmental monitoring.

Spectral investigations were conducted to elucidate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of βCCM-6 in the presence of MG. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MG (red curve) overlapped significantly with the excitation spectrum of βCCM-6 (black curve), indicating the involvement of an inner filter effect (IFE) in the quenching process [68]. Further evidence of fluorescence quenching was obtained from UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 6b). Upon addition of MG, the absorption spectrum of βCCM-6 underwent noticeable changes, indicating strong interactions between MG and βCCM-6 [69]. To determine whether the quenching mechanism was static or dynamic, fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed. Fig. 6c and 6d present the fluorescence decay curves of βCCM-6 before and after MG addition, respectively. The average fluorescence lifetime of βCCM-6 was calculated to be 18.16 ns, which slightly decreased to 17.95 ns after the addition of MG. The minimal change in lifetime indicated that the quenching was predominantly static [70].

The enhanced adsorption and fluorescence detection mechanism of βCCM-6 is schematically illustrated in Fig. 7 . The 3D porous structure of βCCM-6 enhanced its specific surface area and provided a robust framework for efficient MG adsorption [8]. The incorporation of CD@CN and MIL-100(Fe) further enriched the material with abundant active adsorption sites, thereby improving its adsorption capacity [44]. Simultaneously, βCCM-6 played a critical role in stabilizing the fluorescence signal of CD by mitigating self-quenching, while adsorption-induced concentration enrichment amplified the sensitivity to MG [71]. This synergistic interplay between adsorption and fluorescence quenching enabled βCCM-6 to achieve high-efficiency MG detection [71].

In this study, a novel βCCM was synthesized to achieve dual functionalities: adsorption and fluorescence detection. As an adsorbent, βCCM exhibited an exceptionally high adsorption capacity for MG, with a maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of 1789 mg/g. Kinetic and isotherm analyses revealed that the adsorption process followed the pseudo-second-order model and the Langmuir model, indicating chemisorption with monolayer adsorption as the dominant mechanisms. To further understand the adsorption mechanism, FTIR and XPS analyses were conducted. In addition to its impressive adsorption performance, βCCM demonstrated remarkable specificity for MG detection via fluorescence quenching. A strong linear relationship between MG concentration and fluorescence intensity was observed, with an R2 value of 0.9905 and a LOD of 0.09 μg/L. βCCM was successfully applied to detect MG in real water samples, achieving recovery rates between 91.54 % and 109.14 %, with standard deviations below 4.92 %. Spectral investigations provided further insights into the fluorescence detection mechanism, involving both the IFE and static quenching. This study not only highlights the high-performance of βCCM for MG adsorption and detection but also underscores its potential application in environmental monitoring and water treatment.
